title: Punk Rock Egrets: Feathered Mohawks Steal the Show in Smithsonian Photo Contest
summary:
Imagine three tiny birds with feathers sticking up like spiky mohawks, squabbling and pecking at each other like rebellious bandmates onstage. That's exactly what photographer Jacqueline Burke captured in her grand-prize-winning image, "The Punk Rockers," for Smithsonian Magazine's 23rd annual photography contest.

Burke, a 64-year-old veterinarian from Perkiomenville, Pennsylvania, took the photo at a rookery (a bird breeding ground) in Ocean City, New Jersey. The egret chicks, each no larger than a golf ball when curled up, were playing and fighting under a ramp. "Their little necks and heads were writhing," Burke said. "One would peck. The other would peck back." In the background, the parent bird's long legs and enormous feet create a dramatic size contrast, which Smithsonian creative director Maria Keehan called "remarkable."

The contest received more than 17,000 submissions from 108 countries. Among the six other winners: a barefoot devotee expressing herself during the Hindu festival of Shivaratri in Telangana, India, captured by photographer Nalimela; a 14-year-old Navajo girl named DelShay Henio participating in a traditional Kinaaldá ceremony with her horse Hazel, photographed by family friend Carpenter; a rare chameleon in a South African garden, shot by conservation photographer West; and a stunning circular bridge at the Oppo Chang An R&D Center in China, photographed from 500 feet up by Sun.

Burke, who swapped her bird-watching binoculars for a camera in 2014, hopes her image inspires people to protect wildlife. "We need to save species like this and save moments like this," she said. The winning photos are on display in the April/May 2026 issue of Smithsonian magazine.
why_it_matters: Wildlife photography like this raises awareness about species and habitats; egrets face threats from pollution and wetland loss, and a popular photo can draw attention to conservation.

background_read: Smithsonian Magazine’s photo contest began in 2002 and has grown to attract tens of thousands of entries from around the world.
The Smithsonian Institution is the world’s largest museum, education, and research complex, headquartered in Washington, D.C.
Egrets are wading birds that live near wetlands and are protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act in the US.
In recent years, egret populations have recovered from earlier hunting for their plumes, which were used in fashion.
Article_Structure: LEAD: 'Imagine three tiny birds with feathers sticking up like spiky mohawks, squabbling and pecking at each other like rebellious bandmates onstage.'
└─ specific: 'One would peck. The other would peck back.'

├─ WHEN: Photo was entered into the contest (2026 issue).

OPEN QUESTION: Will this image inspire more people to protect bird habitats?

perspectives.description: Photographer Jacqueline Burke feels her photo can help save species. The contest gives her a platform to share her message about protecting wildlife.
Some may argue that a cute photo doesn't lead to real change; the contest is just entertainment, and conservation needs serious policy action, not viral images.
Photo contests are a form of journalism that shape public perception. The Smithsonian’s contest showcases global stories, from birds to cultural ceremonies, offering a snapshot of the world.
Burke started photography in 2014; her success may encourage amateur photographers to document nature. If more people capture similar images, it could build a visual archive of endangered species.
